title: Cat Ravenous Appetite Despite Weight Loss: Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Is your cat always hungry but losing weight? Discover why this happens, the urgency level, and how to help your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Increased appetite paired with weight loss in cats signals a serious metabolic imbalance that requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours to prevent severe complications.

What does it mean if my cat has an increased appetite but is losing weight?

When a cat experiences a ravenous appetite alongside weight loss, it is medically referred to as polyphagia. This condition occurs when the body is either burning calories at an abnormally high rate or is unable to properly absorb and utilize the nutrients from food. Common underlying causes include hyperthyroidism, diabetes mellitus, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), or intestinal parasites.

Is it an emergency if my cat is eating more but losing weight?

  • The urgency level for this condition is Medium.
  • While this is generally not an immediate life-threatening emergency, it signifies a serious metabolic imbalance that requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Delaying treatment can lead to severe muscle wasting, dehydration, or secondary complications like ketoacidosis.

How can taking photos help my veterinarian diagnose my cat's weight loss?

  • Take photos of your cat from a 'top-down' view and a side profile to help the vet assess their Body Condition Score (BCS) and muscle loss.
  • Capture photos of the litter box if you notice changes in their stool.
  • Photograph the feeding area if they are leaving behind undigested food to provide vital diagnostic clues.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with ravenous appetite despite weight loss, hyperthyroidism is a primary differential diagnosis, with weight loss, increased appetite, and hyperexcitability being common signs. Although most hyperthyroid cats have elevated serum total T4 concentrations, a subset may have normal T4 values, especially in mild or early cases, or those with concurrent nonthyroidal illness. Hepatic lipidosis (HL) should also be considered when inappetence is present; appetite stimulants are contraindicated in cats with HL due to the underlying metabolic liver failure.

Chapter: Endocrinology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 559)

faqs.question: Could my cat just have worms?
faqs.answer: Yes, intestinal parasites can steal nutrients, leading to weight loss despite increased hunger. However, in adult cats, metabolic diseases like hyperthyroidism are more common causes.

faqs.question: What diagnostic tests will my vet perform?
faqs.answer: Typically, a vet will start with a 'Senior Screen' or full blood panel, including a T4 test for thyroid function, a blood glucose test for diabetes, and a fecal exam to check for parasites.

faqs.question: Why is my cat acting so frantic for food?
faqs.answer: Diseases like hyperthyroidism increase the metabolic rate, making the cat feel as though they are constantly starving because their body is burning fuel faster than they can consume it.
